# Exercise 1.2

Interpret the augmentation $\varepsilon: \mathbb{Z}G \to \mathbb{Z}$ (i) as a $G$-module homomorphism, (ii) as a morphism in the image of the functor $\mathbb{Z}(\cdot)$.

*Note:* Lemma 1.2 describes the structure of the augmentation ideal $IG = \ker \varepsilon$: as an abelian group $IG$ is free on $\{x-1 \mid 1 \neq x \in G\}$, and as a $G$-module it is generated by $S-1 = \{s-1 \mid s \in S\}$ for any generating set $S$ of $G$. Understanding $\varepsilon$ is key to proving this lemma.
